abstractHtml:
Expanding the repertoire of known retrovirus receptors is of importance in order to unravel and appreciate the complexities of virus tropism and pathogenesis. Known retrovirus receptors can be exploited for targeting retroviruses used as vectors for gene transfer and therapy. Transgenic mice expressing a relevant receptor can be used for both vaccine and drug trials to provide statistically valid results that minimize the use of more expensive and endangered animals. Finally, an understanding of the mechanisms involved in retrovirus entry will allow for the development of agents that can control virus spread at the level of entry.

It is striking that many of the receptors cloned to date utilized by mammalian retroviruses appear to be transporter molecules that span the plasma membrane multiple times. Receptors for the avian viruses, bovine leukaemia virus (BLV) and mouse mammary tumour virus (MMTV) span the plasma membrane once. The human and simian immunodeficiency viruses (HIV/SIV) are currently unique in that the receptor is multicomponent with a principal binding domain that spans the membrane once and a fusion partner that spans the membrane multiple times (Fig. 1). Of the other receptors identified to date it is not yet possible to rule out the potential that these are also multicomponent but that cells lacking these components have not yet been identified. It is tempting to speculate that the presence of multiple membrane-spanning domains in the majority of known retrovirus receptors suggests a common or preferred structural requirement for mediating fusion between viral and cellular membranes in the entry process. Intensive investigations over recent years have highlighted the complexity of virusreceptor interactions, particularly regarding virus tropism. New and somewhat radical interpretations are now emerging which augment earlier perceptions of these interactions.
bodyHtml:
Several new retrovirus receptors have recently been successfully identified and their role in mediating virus entry demonstrated by functional studies. These advances have been facilitated by the improvement of molecular techniques to sift target genes from an overwhelming background of heterologous genetic material. Identifying retrovirus receptors that have eluded recognition for years now puts the field on the precipice of new and exciting findings that will have far reaching consequences within cell and molecular biology as well as gene therapy.

 		Fig. 1. Schematic representation of retrovirus receptor structures. TM, Transmembrane domain; ALV, avian leukosis virus subtypes A, B/D, E; MMTV, mouse mammary tumour virus; BLV, bovine leukaemia virus; MLV, murine leukaemia virus (-E, ecotropic; -A, amphotropic; -X, xenotropic; -P, polytropic); RD114, cat endogenous virus; GALV, gibbon ape leukaemia virus; FeLV-B, feline leukaemia virus subgroup B; FeLV-C, feline leukaemia virus subgroup C; FIV, feline immunodeficiency virus; HIV, human immunodeficiency virus; SIV, simian immunodeficiency virus. * Certain strains of HIV-2 utilize CXCR4 alone.
A virus receptor is conventionally defined as a component(s) on the cell surface to which a virus specifically binds and where this interaction results in virus entry. In contrast, a virus-binding site is a component on the cell surface, which permits virus attachment, but this interaction does not mediate virus internalization. Particle entry is achieved following fusion between retroviral envelope and cellular plasma membranes, the precise details of which remain primarily conjectural. Additional cell surface components (e.g. adhesion molecules) may influence the range of susceptible cells (tropism) by stabilizing virusreceptor interactions and facilitating membrane fusion events.

Retroviral envelope glycoproteins are arranged as oligomers of the globular surface domain (SU) anchored to the viral membrane by the transmembrane domain (TM). For certain viruses SU and TM are disulphide-linked (e.g. Rous sarcoma virus), whereas for other retroviruses (e.g. HIV) they are not disulphide-linked. Following virus attachment, the oligomeric nature of the envelope glycoprotein may favour a clustering of receptor molecules since soluble monomeric glycoproteins can each interact with the receptor. For example, binding of monomeric MMTV gp52 was insufficient to promote receptor internalization, suggesting that aggregation of receptors and oligomeric envelope glycoproteins are important for entry (Bolander, 1996 ). Alternatively, or in addition, sequential binding of envelope glycoproteins may result in receptor aggregation. Virus binding is envisaged to induce a conformational change in SU, which exposes a hydrophobic peptide in the TM domain, which then catalyses fusion between viral and cellular membranes. The process of membrane fusion is not yet fully understood, but by analogy to influenza virus haemagglutinin, fusion may occur by a complex multi-step mechanism that ultimately involves the formation of a fusion pore (Munôz-Barroso et al., 1998 ). Evidence emerging for several retroviruses (e.g. murine leukaemia virus and HIV) suggests that the viral envelope glycoprotein may interact with the receptor twice, first on initial binding and then following conformational change of the envelope glycoprotein. It is also possible that conformational changes in the receptor following virus binding may occur. The conformational change required to trigger exposition of the fusion domain in TM is, for the majority of mammalian and avian retroviruses, a pH-independent reaction (Gilbert et al., 1990 ; McClure et al., 1990 ) where fusion between viral and cellular membranes occurs at the plasma membrane at neutral pH. However, for the ecotropic murine leukaemia virus (MLV-E) and MMTV, this reaction is pH-dependent. Fusion between viral and cellular membranes occurs within an acidified endosomal compartment following internalization via the endocytic pathway (Andersen & Nexø, 1983 ; Bolander, 1996 ). Interestingly, MLV-E entry is pH-independent on rat XC cells. The reasons for this remain obscure and could suggest the presence of factors (e.g. proteases) on XC cells that abrogate the need for low pH to induce viruscell membrane fusion.

The limiting number of receptors required to permit virus entry and/or virus-induced cell fusion is not known and may vary according to the virus. It has been shown that the efficiency of MLV-E infection does not correlate with the level of receptor expression. Cell lines can be poorly sensitive to infection despite the presence of excess receptor. This suggests that other cellular factors might be involved in virus entry and that these factors are differentially expressed according to cell type (Wang et al., 1991b ).

One consequence of virusreceptor interactions is multinucleate giant cell (syncytium) formation. Syncytium formation is a qualitative reaction where the extent of syncytium formation will vary depending upon the virus and the indicator cell line used. It is not clear whether events resulting in fusion between viral and cellular membranes are synonymous with events mediating fusion between cellular membranes in virus-induced syncytium formation. One indication that these processes may be different is that not all susceptible cells will form syncytia on virus infection. Mink lung cells, for example, are sensitive to infection by a wide variety of mammalian viruses but are weakly sensitive to virus-induced syncytium formation (Sommerfelt & Weiss, 1990 ). The reasons for this probably involve aspects including the relative density of receptors at the cell surface, the presence of inhibitors of cell fusion or the lack of promiscuous secondary factors that can interact with several different retrovirus receptors to promote fusion (Siess et al., 1996 ).

Previously, retrovirus receptors were studied as unknown cell surface entities by exploiting the phenomenon of receptor interference (superinfection resistance). Retroviruses demonstrate reciprocal receptor interference when cells chronically infected with one virus remain resistant to superinfection by a virus that uses the same cell surface receptor. The infected cells remain susceptible to infection by viruses that use different receptors. Non-reciprocal interference has been demonstrated by retroviruses of both murine and avian origin. Following identification of the receptors involved, the non-reciprocal interference results from the ability of certain viruses to use more than one receptor.

Syncytium formation is also influenced by receptor interference. Cells chronically infected with viruses that use the same receptor do not induce syncytia when mixed together because their receptors are mutually blocked. Syncytium formation will occur if the cells are infected with viruses that use heterologous receptors. Eleven receptor groups have now been delineated on human cells on the basis of receptor interference studies (Table 1) (Sommerfelt & Weiss, 1990 ; Kewalramani et al., 1992 ; Koo et al., 1992 ; Takeuchi et al., 1998 ). Many of these retrovirus receptors have now been identified and both confirm and explain the cross-interference data (Table 2).

Table 2. Cellular molecules exploited by retroviruses as receptors for entry


Receptor interference is likely to be a predominantly in vitro phenomenon. Chronically infected cells in culture appear to express sufficient envelope glycoproteins at the plasma membrane to block or down-modulate their cognate receptors. HIV can also down-regulate CD4 expression by virtue of its regulatory proteins such as Nef and Vpu. It is unclear whether receptor interference is ever fully established in vivo, with the exception of endogenous viruses that are xenotropic due to the expression of envelope glycoproteins at the cell surface which confer superinfection resistance (reviewed by Weiss, 1993 ). Following infection by exogenous viruses, not all receptor molecules at the cell surface may become occupied or be down-modulated by viral envelope glycoproteins. Envelope glycoprotein synthesis compared to receptor gene expression may also vary. For HIV infection this means that individuals may be infected with multiple virus strains where co-infection can lead to the development of recombinant viruses. The ecotropic murine leukaemia retrovirus receptor
The MLV-E receptor was the first retrovirus receptor to be molecularly cloned using gene-transfer techniques (Table 2) (Albritton et al., 1989 ). The receptor (MCAT) proved to be an integral membrane protein that spanned the plasma membrane 14 times. It functioned as a sodium-independent basic amino acid transporter of lysine, arginine and ornithine (Kim et al., 1991 ; Wang et al., 1991a ). The gene encoding MCAT was found to reside within the same genetic locus as previously reported, confirming the earlier gene assignment (Kozak et al., 1990 ) (Table 2). Occupancy of the receptor by envelope glycoproteins in chronically infected cells reduces the efficacy of amino acid transport (Wang et al., 1992 ). Interestingly, a mutation of MCAT has been generated that abrogates amino acid transport without affecting virus-receptor function, providing evidence that these two functions are unlinked (Wang et al., 1994 ).

Although MLV-E infects only mouse and certain rat cells, a human homologue (HCAT) has also been identified. HCAT shows 87% identity to MCAT and has been mapped to human chromosome 13q12-q14 (Yoshimoto et al., 1990 ; Albritton et al., 1992 ). Differences between the non-functional human and functional murine homologues have been used to localize the region(s) important for MLV-E receptor activity on MCAT. Two amino acids on the third extracellular loop of MCAT, YGE(235237) compared to PGV(242244) on HCAT, were required to confer receptor function (Albritton et al., 1993 ). In this system, only one strain of replication-competent MLV-E and one replication-defective strain was used. It is conceivable that the regions important for receptor function may vary according to the virus strain used, as has been noted for other virus-receptor systems.

The gibbon ape leukaemia virus/feline leukaemia virus subgroup B receptor and the amphotropic murine leukaemia virus receptor
Receptor interference between feline leukaemia virus subgroup B (FeLV-B), gibbon ape leukaemia virus (GALV) and woolly monkey sarcoma virus (WMSV), previously known as simian sarcoma-associated virus, was first observed using syncytial assays (Sommerfelt & Weiss, 1990 ). Superinfection resistance using pseudotype viruses based on recombinant Moloney murine leukaemia virus (Takeuchi et al., 1992 ) later confirmed this. The GALV receptor gene, Glvr, was cloned in 1990 (OHara et al., 1990 ) and proved to be sodium-dependent inorganic phosphate symporter (Kavanaugh et al., 1994 ; Olah et al., 1994 ) that was predicted to carry ten TMs and five extracellular loops. The GALV receptor, Pit1, conferred sensitivity to infection by FeLV-B, confirming the interference data. By exploiting differences between the functional human Pit1 and the non-functional mouse homologue, the binding sites on the receptor for GALV and FeLV-B were localized to the fourth extracellular loop and clustered within a region of nine amino acids termed Region A (Johann et al., 1993 ; Tailor et al., 1993 ). The amino acid sequence within this region was polymorphic between species, resulting in altered susceptibility to these viruses. This suggests that the receptor binding site as a defined amino acid sequence is not conserved between species and that sequence differences in the receptor contribute to control of virus tropism.

When the gene encoding the amphotropic murine leukaemia virus (MLV-A) receptor was eventually cloned it showed significant identity to that of the GALV receptor and, like the GALV receptor, functioned as a sodium-dependent inorganic phosphate transporter (Miller et al., 1994 ; van Zeijl et al., 1994 ) (Table 2). Identifying Pit1 and Pit2 as the receptors for GALV/FeLV-B and MLV-A respectively allowed an explanation for the non-reciprocal interference noted between MLV-A 10A1 and GALV, where MLV-A 10A1 could use both Pit1 and Pit2 for entry whilst GALV could only use Pit1.

Considering both the structural (62% identity) and functional similarity between Pit1 and Pit2, extensive mutagenic analysis has been undertaken to determine regions on the receptors necessary for receptor function for their cognate viruses and to explain the observed receptor interference (discussed in Lundorf et al., 1999 ). Chimeric proteins of Pit1 and Pit2 have also been derived which provide susceptibility to either GALV, FeLV-B and/or MLV-A (Pedersen et al., 1995 ). Interestingly, the chimeric proteins retain their transporter function. Recently, an interesting study showed that loops 2 and 4 of Pit2 were required for MLV-A entry. Different combinations of amino acid sequences from loops 2 and 4 could fulfil receptor function. Pit2 shows 25% amino acid identity to Pho-4, a sodium-dependent phosphate transporter of Neurospora crassa (Johann et al., 1992 ). Transferring Pit2 loops 2 and 4 into Pho-4 conferred MLV-A receptor activity to Pho-4. This showed that the transporter backbone of Pho-4 was sufficient to present the Pit loops 2 and 4 extracellular domains in a manner that allowed the chimera to function as a receptor for MLV-A (Lundorf et al., 1999 ).

The relative simplicity of mapping the domains on Pit1 recognized by FeLV-B and GALV compared to sometimes conflicting results on Pit2 for MLV-A may be because insufficient virus strains have been analysed. In addition to important sites on the receptor, regions of the viral envelope will also influence receptor interactions (Boomer et al., 1997 ; Tailor & Kabat, 1997 ).

Both Pit1 and Pit2 are widely expressed in vivo, yet the viruses that use these receptors are predominantly associated with leukaemia; WMSV, being the exception, causes sarcomas. This suggests that other factors post-entry may contribute to pathogenic effects.

The xenotropic and polytropic murine leukaemia virus receptor
The xenotropic murine leukaemia virus (MLV-X) receptor gene has recently been independently cloned by three groups (Battini et al., 1999 ;Tailor et al., 1999a ; Yang et al., 1999 ) and is specific for MLV-X and the polytropic murine leukaemia virus (P-MLV) (previously mink cell focus-forming virus). The shared utilization explains patterns of receptor interference observed for these viruses on murine cells. The X-receptor is highly related to the yeast proteins SYG1, of unknown function, and also PHO81, which has been implicated in phosphate transport. Thus, in common with MLV-A and GALV/FeLV-B receptors, the X-receptor appears to be a multiple membrane-spanning transporter molecule.

The human, simian and feline immunodeficiency virus receptors
CD4, the first retrovirus receptor to be identified as a predefined cell surface molecule was initially described as an essential component of the HIV receptor (Dalgleish et al., 1984 ). This was prudent since 2 years later it became evident that CD4 did not function alone, and that additional molecules were required to generate a functional HIV receptor complex (Maddon et al., 1986 ). HIV-1, HIV-2 and SIV exhibited subtle differences in tropism, suggesting that they had the capacity to exploit distinct molecules as co-receptors (Clapham et al., 1991 ; Dragic & Alizon, 1993 ).

Finally, after 10 years of intensive research, two components were independently identified that permitted HIV membrane fusion in conjunction with CD4. These were the α chemokine receptor CXCR4 (Feng et al., 1996 ) and the β chemokine receptor CCR5 (Deng et al., 1996 ). Co-expression of CD4 and CXCR4 allowed both binding and entry of predominantly T-cell tropic HIV-1 strains (also generalized as rapid/high viruses or syncytium-inducing viruses on MT-2 cells). Co-expression of CCR5 and CD4, however, conferred susceptibility to predominantly macrophage-tropic primary HIV-1 strains (generally the slow/low viruses and non-syncytium-inducing on MT-2 cells). Later, other chemokine receptors were found to be used by certain HIV primary strains in addition to either CXCR4 or CCR5 (Doranz et al., 1996 ) (Table 2).

Shortly after the identification of CXCR4 and CCR5 for HIV-1 entry, other chemokine receptors were similarly described for SIV and HIV-2 (Deng et al., 1997 ; Owen et al., 1998 ) (Table 2). All the chemokine receptors described span the plasma membrane seven times and are G-protein-coupled receptors where their downstream G-protein signal transduction pathways are reasonably well understood.

For certain strains of HIV-2 CD4 is dispensable for infection; however, entry is enhanced in the presence of soluble CD4 (sCD4) (Clapham et al., 1992 ). It is now evident that these viruses enter by virtue of CXCR4 alone (Endres et al., 1996 ). The host range of such HIV-2 strains is expanded since CXCR4 is present on a wide variety of CD4- cell types in vitro.

In common with studies of other retrovirus receptors approaches involving mutagenesis of CXCR4 and generating chimeric chemokine receptors have been undertaken to try and delineate the regions of CXCR4 necessary for HIV interaction. The results have been conflicting, which has also highlighted the extreme variation between the interactions of virus strains with chemokine receptors (reviewed by Moore et al., 1997 ; Doms & Piepert, 1997 ; Berger, 1997 ).

A receptor for feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) was previously reported to be CD9, a molecule that spans the membrane four times. This was based on the availability of an antibody that blocked FIV infection (Hosie et al., 1993 ; Willett et al., 1994 ). Later work suggested that the observed inhibitory effects of antibodies to CD9 affected FIV infection at a stage that was post-entry (De Parseval et al., 1997 ; Willett et al., 1997b ). Recently, evidence was presented that FIV could use both the feline and human homologues of CXCR4 alone (Willett et al., 1997c ). No information is currently available as to whether FIV and HIV-2 strains using CXCR4 alone exhibit reciprocal receptor interference.

CXCR4 has been presented as a potential common lentivirus receptor (Willett et al., 1997a ). Interestingly, CXCR4 shows striking sequence identity between species, reflected by observations that CXCR4 molecules from diverse species can function as FIV receptors with varying efficiency. The failure of FIV to establish infection in rats and humans must therefore lie in other parameters of in vivo infection, such as inactivation by human complement. CXCR4 does not appear to be a co-receptor utilized by SIV strains, with the exception of SIVmnd(GB-1) (Schols & De Clercq, 1998 ).

It has been tempting to speculate that since such a variety of lentiviruses use CXCR4, even independently of CD4, this molecule represents a primordial lentivirus receptor and that the need for CD4 was acquired later in evolution. CCR5 is the receptor utilized by HIV strains associated with asymptomatic infection, where a requirement for CXCR4 could result from virus evolution in vivo. It is therefore puzzling that CXCR4 should be a primordial receptor and not CCR5. A primordial lentivirus receptor role for CXCR4 can only be resolved once the importance of this molecule in the entry of all Lentivirus members (e.g. the ovine, caprine, bovine lentiviruses) has been determined.

Following binding to CD4, the HIV surface glycoprotein undergoes a conformational change and this altered conformation interacts with a chemokine receptor. Studies are currently in progress to find agents that inhibit HIV interactions with CXCR4 or CCR5. However, to exert any inhibitory activity they may have to act post-CD4 binding. Making drugs that will inhibit interactions with only CXCR4 or CCR5 may result in the selection of strains that utilize other co-receptors. It will be necessary to establish that therapies based on sCD4 will not act as enhancing agents, since sCD4 can induce conformational changes in Env that may promote entry via chemokine receptors rather than inhibit it. Designing peptides that block the CD4-binding site on gp120 to prevent conformational changes may be more appropriate and effective against diverse virus strains. The nature of the virus-binding site on CD4, however, may not be readily accessible to such agents. CXCR4 and CD4 co-expressed on two rhabdoviruses has been suggested as a means to seek out and kill HIV-infected cells (Mebatsion et al., 1997 ; Schnell et al., 1997 ). It remains to be seen how suitable such a therapeutic approach can be. Other targeted vector systems carrying toxins may be more appropriate.

Due to its exploitation by HIV and FIV CXCR4 has been implicated as a key determinant in the pathogenesis of AIDS (Willett et al., 1997a ). This tallies with observations that X4 viruses (those that utilize CXCR4) are associated with the onset of AIDS. However, it is becoming increasingly apparent that not all patients exhibiting full blown AIDS actually develop X4 viruses (e.g. patients infected with HIV subgroup C). It is conceivable that CXCR4 may be incorporated onto virus particles during budding from the cell surface. A consequence of this may be to distort the natural homeostasis of CXCR4 receptor expression in vivo which in turn will affect chemokine function and may inadvertently contribute to pathogenesis.

Type D/RD114/baboon endogenous virus/avian reticuloendotheliosis retrovirus receptor
As shown in Table 1, the type D retroviruses exhibit reciprocal receptor interference with the type C retroviruses, baboon endogenous virus (BaEV), RD114 and the avian reticuloendotheliosis viruses (REV) (Sommerfelt & Weiss, 1990 ; Kewalramani et al., 1992 ; Koo et al., 1992 ).

The exogenous type D retroviruses are subtyped according to neutralization specificity. That viruses having distinct neutralization specificities bind the same cell surface receptor could suggest that either they utilize distinct epitopes of the same cell surface receptor or that neutralization occurs as a post-binding event involving regions of the receptor other than the virus-binding site. The receptor shared by this large group of retroviruses took many years to identify. By exploiting the neutralization epitope of simian retrovirus serotype 1 (SRV-1), a molecule of 60 kDa was identified as a putative receptor on Raji cells (Benjamini et al., 1991 ; Torres et al., 1991 ). The gene encoding the receptor for the RD114/BaEV/Type D/REV receptor interference groups, however, has only recently been independently cloned by two groups. Rasko et al. (1999) showed receptor function for RD114 and spleen necrosis virus (SNV), whereas Tailor et al. (1999b ) provided evidence that the receptor also conferred susceptibility to infection by the type D retroviruses. The receptor gene is synonymous with the hATB0 gene, which encodes a sodium-dependent amino acid transporter. HATB0 is a multiple membrane-spanning glycoprotein that traverses the plasma membrane ten times. The receptor has a predicted molecular mass of 57 kDa, which is similar in size to that of the 60 kDa putative receptor previously identified. Whether these two molecules are the same remains to be determined.

On infection the activity of this transporter is impaired, a feature that has been suggested as a partial explanation for the pathogenesis of these viruses, although none of the endogenous viruses are pathogenic in their hosts. The endogenous viruses of this receptor group show a xenotropic host range, implying that the receptors in the host species are not available, probably due to blockage by envelope gene expression. Other transporter molecules may compensate for deficiencies in amino acid transport in these infected hosts. Since the endogenous type D retroviruses are found in both Old and New World primates it is evident that these viruses are ancient, yet remarkably, their receptor specificity has been maintained. These viruses are known to be less variable than HIV and SIV. Regarding the endogenous retroviruses of this receptor group, the continued presence of these viruses within the host genome may provide a specific advantage that supersedes any deficiency in amino acid transport. It is interesting that viruses associated with both leukaemia (cell proliferation), such as MLV-E, and immunosuppression (cell death), such as the type D retroviruses, have receptors that carry out amino acid transport.

The type D receptor is different from that used by HIV/SIV, yet both the exogenous type D retroviruses and HIV/SIV cause AIDS. For the type D retroviruses this is exemplified in a depletion of both CD4 and CD8 cells compared to HIV/SIV, where CD4 cell counts are reduced. The different forms of immunosuppression may reflect the use of different receptors or alternatively lie in domains associated with envelope glycoproteins.

It is only now that the receptor used by this large interference group has been identified that these molecules can be compared and contrasted between species in order to try and understand differences in tropism observed within this receptor group and compared with HIV/SIV to determine what role the receptor has in inducing immunosuppression.

Feline leukaemia virus subgroup C receptors
FeLV subgroups A, B and C utilize distinct receptors. FeLV-B and -C are derived from FeLV-A-infected cats following either mutation (for FeLV-C) or recombination with endogenous retroviral sequences (for FeLV-B). FeLV-B and -C have acquired an altered receptor specificity compared to FeLV-A and an expanded host range, which includes human cells. A putative FeLV-A receptor on cat cells has been identified by immunoprecipitation as a 70 kDa molecule (Ghosh et al., 1992 ).

Recently, a putative cell surface receptor for FeLV-C was identified as a member of a transporter superfamily (Tailor et al., 1999c ). The receptor (FLVCR) carries 12 hydrophobic potential TMs and appears to belong to the major-facilitator superfamily (MFS) of transporters. The FLVCR is most closely related to the organic phosphate antiporter and anion/cation symporter subfamilies, both of which transport organic anions. It is not known whether the FeLV-A receptor is related to the FeLV-C receptor, although the molecular mass of the putative FeLV-A receptor is consistent with the size of MFS transporters. Further characterization of the putative FeLV-A receptor gene is required to resolve this issue.

Human immunodeficiency virus
The glycolipid galactosyl ceramide (GalCer) expressed on neuronal cells and the intestinal epithelium has been presented as an alternative receptor for HIV-1, explaining CD4-independent entry into these cells in vitro (Bhat et al., 1991 ; Harouse et al., 1991 ; Yahi et al., 1992 ). Later, the region of gp120 that binds GalCer was localized to a region distinct from the CD4-binding domain (Bhat et al., 1993 ). Bona fide entry mediated by GalCer alone is difficult to demonstrate using molecular techniques because synthesis of GalCer involves many complicated biosynthetic pathways. Receptor function by GalCer is therefore implied, since susceptibility to infection correlates with expression levels of GalCer in colon and intestinal epithelia (Fantini et al., 1993 ) and on liposomes (Long et al., 1994 ).

HIV infection of colon epithelia in vitro is clearly CD4-independent. However, before GalCer can be deemed an independent alternative receptor, the role of CXCR4, known to be expressed on colon epithelia (Zhang et al., 1998 ), in HIV entry will have to be determined. GalCer clearly interacts with gp120, but it is not clear if this interaction is sufficient to trigger the necessary conformational changes needed for CXCR4-mediated fusion of viral and cellular membranes. The importance of GalCer in HIV entry remains predominantly an in vitro observation since neuronal cells and intestinal epithelia are rarely infected in vivo. Binding of HIV envelope glycoproteins to GalCer alone, however, may contribute to pathogenesis, for example in the gastrointestinal disorders associated with HIV infection and in neuronal dysfunction in AIDS dementia.

The receptor for bovine leukaemia virus
BLV is a B-lymphotropic virus and, like the related human T-lymphotropic viruses (HTLV), the receptor seems to be widespread over many tissues and across many species. Neither BLV nor HTLV exhibits receptor interference and so it is anticipated that these viruses use distinct receptors (Table 1). Two cDNA clones (BLVRcp1 and BLVRcp1/5') corresponding to the bovine BLV receptor gene have been generated (Ban et al., 1993 , 1994 ) that appear to encode a protein that spans the plasma membrane once and has a molecular mass of 94·2 kDa. The extracellular amino-terminal domain binds the viral envelope glycoprotein gp51. Interestingly, the murine homologue of the BLV receptor carries upstream sequences that are absent from the bovine receptor gene (Suzuki & Ikeda, 1998 ) and mRNA corresponding to the bovine BLV receptor is longer than the two cDNA clones isolated to date (Ban et al., 1993 , 1994 ). Taken together this suggests that the bovine BLV receptor gene cloned to date is incomplete. Surprisingly, the murine homologue carries a termination codon prior to the predicted TM and appears more related to the δ subunit of the AP-3 adapter-related protein complex, which localizes to the cytoplasm (Suzuki & Ikeda, 1998 ). This could explain why mice are resistant to BLV infection. Clearly further work is required to understand fully the nature of the BLV receptor on bovine cells and on cells from other species that show differential susceptibility to BLV infection.

The avian sarcoma and leukosis viruses
The avian sarcoma and leukosis viruses (ASLV) can be divided into five major subgroups designated A to E based on patterns of receptor interference. Sugroups A and C utilize distinct receptors, whilst B and D retroviruses exhibit non-reciprocal receptor interference with subgroup E. The genes conferring susceptibility to infection have been assigned to alleles of three autosomal loci, tv-a, tv-b and tv-c. Receptors for subgroup A and C viruses lie within the tv-a and tv-c loci respectively, whilst the tv-b locus carries alleles that encode receptors for subgroups B, D and E viruses.

A receptor specific for subgroup A ASLV was identified by gene-cloning techniques and conferred susceptibility to infection in mammalian cells. The receptor was designated TVA and showed significant relatedness to the ligand-binding domain of the human low density lipoprotein receptor (LDLR) (Young et al., 1993 ; Bates et al., 1993 ). The region encompassing the LDLR repeat motif (spanning 40 amino acids) within which two acidic amino acid residues and an aromatic one are found (at positions 46, 47 and 48 respectively) is sufficient to generate the functional receptor (Rong & Bates, 1995 ; Zingler et al., 1995 ). The cloned receptor gene has since been mapped to the tv-a locus on the chicken genome (Bates et al., 1998 ). The alleles conferring susceptibility (TV-A*S) and resistance (TV-A*R) appear to be distinct but do not differ in the receptor binding domain. The basis of resistance is therefore intriguing and remains to be determined. The precise function of the receptor and the identity of its ligand is unknown. Alternative splicing can lead to a secreted form of the receptor that can neutralize virus and prevent infection; whether this occurs in vivo is not known (Connolly et al., 1994 ).

The cytopathic avian leukosis virus (ALV)-B and -D and the non-cytopathic ALV-E were predicted to use similar cellular receptors encoded by the tv-b locus. A set of five different alleles of the tv-b locus have been identified. tv-bs1, tv-bs2a and tv-bs2b permit infection by ASLV subgroups B, D and E, whilst tv-bs3 allows infection by ALV-B and -D only and tv-br does not permit entry by any of these viruses. The tv-bs3 gene has been cloned and encodes the receptor (CAR1) for ASLV subgroups B and D and appears to be a member of the tumour necrosis factor receptor (TNFR) family. The predicted cytoplasmic tail of CAR1 contains a region strikingly similar to the defined death domains of Fas and TNFR1, which are important for apoptosis induction. On cross-linking by envelope glycoproteins, CAR1 induces apoptosis. This was considered as one explanation for why subgroups B and D were cytopathic (Brojatch et al., 1996 ). The gene encoding CAR1 has recently been mapped to the tv-b locus (Smith et al., 1998 ).

The non-reciprocal receptor interference exhibited by avian viruses B/D and E suggests that the ALV-E receptor is similar to the B/D receptor. Following cloning of the subgroup E ALV receptor (SEAR) from turkey cells, a molecule related to CAR1 was identified that also carries a cytoplasmic death domain in common with other members of the TNFR family (Adkins et al., 1997 ). ALV-E, however, is non-cytopathic, so the importance of the death domain is therefore unclear for this virus. It remains to be seen whether ALV-E interactions with SEAR induce apoptosis, or whether subgroups B and D can also use SEAR. The gene encoding SEAR remains to be allocated to a defined tv locus.

Mouse mammary tumour virus
In 1991, a protein was purified from mammary epithelial cells that bound MMTV envelope glycoprotein. Antibodies to this protein blocked MMTV infection, implicating this protein as a putative receptor (Bolander & Blackstone, 1991 ; Bolander, 1996 ). Recently, a gene encoding susceptibility to infection by MMTV was cloned (Golovkina et al., 1998 ). The receptor proved to be more widely expressed than the tropism of MMTV, suggesting that post-penetration host control of replication may play a role in MMTV tropism or that a secondary factor, more restricted in its expression, is important for mediating virus entry. Although previously mapped to mouse chromosome 16 using VSV(MMTV) pseudotypes (Hilkens et al., 1983 ), the cloned receptor gene maps to the proximal end of mouse chromosome 19. The discrepancy in chromosome localization is puzzling. It is unlikely that two different factors encoded by separate chromosomes can independently mediate virus entry as this would have made chromosome assignment extremely difficult (a chromosome assignment for the secondary factors involved in HIV entry was never achieved despite using somatic cell hybrids that retained chromosome 12 encoding CD4). One explanation for the observed discrepancy is that for the somatic cell hybrids originally used, a small region of chromosome 19 carrying the receptor gene may have translocated to chromosome 16. The natural function of the MMTV receptor remains to be determined and it will be interesting to ascertain whether the protein previously identified and the cloned receptor are the same.

Human T-lymphotropic viruses 1 and 2
The identity of the receptor shared by HTLV-1 and HTLV-2 remains an enigma despite extensive study. The HTLV receptor gene was provisionally assigned to human chromosome 17q and further localized to 17q23.2-25.3 using humanrodent hybrid cells (Sommerfelt et al., 1988 ; Gavalchin et al., 1995 ; Li et al., 1996 ). Interestingly, this coincided with a region on chromosome 17 encoding the antigen recognized by monoclonal antibody 34-23 that blocks HTLV-1 entry (Gavalchin et al., 1993 , 1995 ). Several other candidate receptors of varying molecular masses have also been identified, primarily as antigens to antibodies that block HTLV-1 syncytium formation (Imai et al., 1992 ; Imai & Yoshie, 1993 ; Agadjanyan et al., 1998 ; Fu et al., 1998 ; Sagara et al., 1998 ). With the antibodies that inhibit virus-induced syncytium formation or infection, one cannot rule out the possibility that the effects are steric and that the receptor co-localizes with the antigen recognized by the antibody in question (Hildreth, 1998 ). For all the aforementioned candidate receptors isolation of the relevant cDNA and transfection into a suitable system (e.g. micelles) where receptor function can be demonstrated has not been reported.

The vascular cell adhesion molecule-1 (VCAM-1) has been implicated in HTLV-1-mediated cell fusion as potentially comprising the receptor or a component of the receptor in certain transfected cell lines (Hildreth et al., 1997 ). However, certain cell lines lacking VCAM-1 expression are susceptible to HTLV infection. The gene encoding VCAM-1 is located on chromosome 1p31-p32. The importance of VCAM-1 in HTLV-2 infection has not been addressed, and would be interesting since these viruses demonstrate receptor interference.

A complicating factor regarding the HTLV receptor has recently been highlighted following the development of high titre pseudotypes that fail to identify cell lines that lack HTLV receptors. This suggests that the HTLV receptor is ubiquitous for all animal species tested (Sutton & Littman, 1996 ). Similarly, other assay systems based on high level expression of HTLV envelope glycoproteins have shown that the range of cells that express HTLV receptors is greater than previously anticipated (Okuma et al., 1999 ). These findings have questioned the chromosome assignment of the HTLV receptor gene to human chromosome 17. At present, the potential for artificially high level expression of HTLV glycoproteins on pseudotypes or cell lines resulting in an artificially high (and potentially non-specific) level of virus binding and entry cannot be ruled out. Mouse cells do express HTLV receptors; however, the presence of the relevant human chromosomal DNA in humanmouse somatic cell hybrids was sufficient to increase the susceptibility to virus entry over 100-fold (Sommerfelt et al., 1988 ). Several independent investigators using different viruses (including HTLV-2) and different somatic cell hybrids have obtained the same result (Gavalchin et al., 1995 ; Li et al., 1996 ), suggesting that chromosome 17 does encode a factor that significantly improves susceptibility to infection.

It is evident that the molecules involved in HTLV entry are highly complex. None of the candidate receptors presented thus far are expressed as widely and across as many animal species as the HTLV receptor. The potential that HTLV can use different receptors according to species cannot be ruled out. Furthermore, despite a broad receptor host range, pathogenesis appears limited to T-cells, suggesting other factors control pathogenesis. It is clear that the conundrum concerning HTLV entry will only be resolved following identification of the gene(s) conferring susceptibility to infection.

The non-primate lentiviruses
Maedivisna virus (MVV) of sheep still represents the prototype lentivirus yet the nature of the MVV receptor remains unclear. Ovine class II major histocompatibility complex (MHC) was presented as a component of the receptor based on evidence that soluble ovine class II MHC could inhibit MVV infection. Antibodies to ovine MHC class II, however, only partially blocked infection (Dalziel et al., 1991 ). A molecule of 50 kDa was also identified as a putative MVV receptor, where antibodies to this protein inhibited virus binding (Crane et al., 1991 ). No further characterization of this molecule has been made.

The ovine and caprine viruses (MVV and caprine arthritisencephalitis virus, CAEV) do not exhibit reciprocal receptor interference, suggesting that their receptors are distinct (Jolly & Narayan, 1989 ). The nature of the receptors for equine infectious anaemia virus of horses, bovine immunodeficiency virus (BIV) of domestic cattle, Jembrana disease virus of Bali cattle, and the lentiviruses associated with pumas and lions (which are closely related to FIV) are not known. The importance of CXCR4 as a receptor for these lentiviruses has not been addressed and will depend on the availability of suitable culture systems for virus propagation. The fact that MVV and CAEV use distinct receptors weakens the potential for CXCR4 to represent a common lentivirus receptor. Although the majority of lentivirus receptors are not known, members of this genus primarily infect cells of the monocyte/macrophage lineage in vivo. MVV, CAEV and BIV can also grow in certain fibroblastic cultures in vitro (e.g. MVV can be propagated in ovine, caprine, bovine, canine, porcine and human choroid plexus cells), showing that their receptors are expressed across species barriers. Not all lentiviruses show species-specific pathogenicity, since BIV can produce immune defects in experimentally infected rabbits. It is clear that much remains to be learned regarding the receptors for the non-primate lentiviruses.

The porcine endogenous retroviruses
A new receptor interference group on human cells has recently been established (Takeuchi et al., 1998 ). Renewed interest in porcine retroviruses stems from safety considerations for the use of pig tissues in organ xenotransplantation to humans. Although endogenous, these viruses can be activated following mitogenic stimulation, a feature consistent with a response to the foreign organ in the transplant recipient (Wilson et al., 1998 ). Furthermore, fresh endothelial cells from pigs have been shown to spontaneously produce porcine endogenous retrovirus (PERV) (Martin et al., 1998 ).

Three subclasses of PERV have been identified that can be distinguished on the basis of their envelope genes, host range and distinct receptor requirement (Table 1). Human cells express receptors for all current PERV subtypes. PERV A and PERV B have a wider host range and can infect several human cell lines, whilst PERV C has a restricted host range infecting pig and only certain human cell lines. The host range of PERV C can be expanded following phenotypic mixing (pseudotype formation) in cells co-infected with PERV B or PERV C, a feature likely to occur in vivo. Potential also exists for recombination between PERV C and fragments of other PERVs present in the same cell.

Interestingly, non-human primate cells appear resistant to PERV, implying that the receptors are not conserved in the primate lineage or that the receptor binding site is blocked due to secondary modifications. This precludes the use of primates in determining the importance of these viruses in xenotransplantation. Since cells from the same species differ in sensitivity to PERV infection this suggests that either the receptors are not constitutively expressed on all cell types or that they are polymorphic in their recognition sites with PERV Env. The fact that PERVs can infect pig cells suggests that they have an amphotropic host range and may establish active infection in pig tissues transferred to humans (Patience et al., 1997 ). Identifying the receptors used by these viruses is important to enable the development of intervention strategies that can impede infection in recipients of organ transplants from infected pigs.

Virus receptors are cell-encoded molecules that are exploited by viruses to mediate virus entry, an event that may or may not affect their natural function. Of the receptors identified to date it appears that the natural function of the receptor is independent of its function as a virus receptor, although the natural function may be impaired (Atchison et al., 1996 ; Rasko et al., 1999 ; Olah et al., 1994 ; Wang et al., 1992 ).

The ability of surface molecules to function as receptors is dependent upon their cellular environment. Virus tropism will be influenced by the degree of receptor identity between host species and differences in post-translational modifications (glycosylation) between species. Secondary factors expressed on certain cell types that function in association with diverse receptors to mediate fusion may influence tropism.

Mutagenesis of known retrovirus receptors has shown clearly how small amino acid differences can affect receptor function. For example, only two amino acid changes are required to confer receptor function to the human homologue of MLV-E or to inactivate the murine receptor (Albritton et al., 1993 ; Yoshimoto et al., 1993 ). Substitution of a single amino acid at position 552 of the fourth extracellular region is sufficient to render Pit2 an MLV-A receptor functional as a GALV receptor (Eiden et al., 1996 ). However, for other receptor components such as the chemokine receptor for HIV and Pit2 for MLV-A, the sequences important for receptor function may be strain-dependent, illustrating how virusreceptor interactions are also intimately linked to the structure of the envelope glycoproteins. Furthermore, the overall surface topology of the receptor may be the feature that confers receptor function. A similar surface topology can be composed of different amino acid sequences. Virusreceptor interactions should lead to the relevant conformational changes in envelope glycoproteins (and receptor?) that will promote membrane fusion.

BaEV has a low plating efficiency on mouse cells in contrast to the cat endogenous virus RD114 that uses the same cell surface receptor (Table 1). Treatment of mouse cells with tunicamycin, however, renders them susceptible to RD114 (Blair et al., 1988 ), showing that the RD114 receptor is expressed on mouse cells but that epitopes for virus binding are unavailable due to glycosylation. Similar results have been obtained for REV and type D retroviruses (Koo et al., 1994 ). Inhibition of glycosylation has increased cellular susceptibility to infection by a number of viruses, e.g. CD4-independent infection of HIV-2 on human cells (Talbot et al., 1995 ) and MLV-E infection of hamster cells (Wang et al., 1996 ).

It is clear that in addition to host transcriptional factors affecting viral gene expression, cellular receptors constitute a major determinant of virus tropism. Tropism can, however, be influenced by other factors and the importance of other cellular molecules as accessory factors in virus entry must not be underestimated. Previous studies have shown that certain antibodies against CD18, a component of the leukocyte function antigen-1 (LFA-1) could inhibit HIV-1-induced syncytium formation (Hildreth & Orentas, 1989 ; Pantaleo et al., 1991 ). Interestingly, LFA-1 represents the natural ligand for the intercellular adhesion molecules ICAM-1, ICAM-2 and ICAM-3. When HIV-1 is released from infected cells by budding, cellular proteins, particularly adhesion molecules such as LFA-1 and ICAM-1, can become incorporated in the viral envelope together with HIV envelope glycoproteins. These cellular molecules can thereby stabilize virusreceptor interactions by binding their cognate receptors concomitantly with HIV gp120 binding CD4 and in this way influence tropism (Guo & Hildreth, 1995 ). Adhesion molecules have been shown to be incorporated into the envelope of HTLV-1 (Akari et al., 1995 ; Hildreth et al., 1997 ). Antibodies to ICAM-1, ICAM-2 and ICAM-3 together will inhibit HIV-1-induced syncytium formation (Butini et al., 1994 ). Similarly, certain antibodies to ICAM-3 alone will also significantly inhibit HIV-1 entry, but have varying effects on syncytium formation (Sommerfelt & sjö, 1995 ). In addition to adhesion molecules, MHC are also incorporated into virions (Akari et al., 1995 ). The incorporation of cellular molecules into virion membranes exemplifies how the cellular origin of the virus can influence virus tropism and may also play a role in virus pathogenesis.

Cellular proteases have been implicated in virus entry originally based on observations that trypsin could enhance MLV-E entry (Andersen, 1987 ) and that the V3 loop of the HIV envelope glycoprotein gp120 contained sites suitable for cleavage by proteolytic enzymes, e.g. tryptase, thrombin, cathepsin E (Clements et al., 1991 ) and CD26 (Callebaut et al., 1993 ). In 1993 the cell surface proteinase CD26 was reported to be the co-receptor shared by HIV-1 and HIV-2 (Callebaut et al., 1993 ). However, this did not reconcile observations showing distinct co-factor requirements for HIV-1 and HIV-2 (Clapham et al., 1991 ). A co-receptor function for CD26 was later deemed unlikely (Lazaro et al., 1994 ). Although such cleavage sites and events have been reported in vitro, their biological relevance in vivo remains to be determined.

HIV and HTLV entry can be inhibited by sulphated polysaccharides such as dextran sulphate and heparin in vitro. These agents also inhibit unrelated enveloped viruses such as vesicular stomatitis virus (VSV), herpes simplex virus and cytomegalovirus, suggesting a common mechanism of inhibition for enveloped viruses (Baba et al., 1988 ; Haraguchi et al., 1997 ). Their effect is early in infection as they are applied before the addition of virus. Interestingly, dextran sulphate inhibits serine proteinases and so theoretically can inhibit the role of this protease in virus entry. Wheat germ agglutinin (Yang et al., 1994 ) and phospholipids (Yang et al., 1997 ) also inhibit HTLV infection, probably in a non-specific manner. Lack of virus specificity does not rule out the importance of these agents for future therapeutic advantage, although toxicity studies would have to determine their suitability for any potential clinical application.

To date, HIV represents the only retrovirus whose principal receptor is definitively known to be comprised of more than one component. This raises the possibility that other retrovirus receptors are also multicomponent but that cells lacking one or more of these components have not yet been identified. Some retroviruses have a very wide host range, which would suggest that functional homologues of the receptor components are widely distributed on cells from many species. For the HIV and SIV strains showing CD4-dependent entry, CD4 utilization is conserved despite co-receptor flexibility. This suggests that the principal binding site of the receptor CD4 and not components of the receptor affecting fusion determine the observed receptor interference between these viruses.

The multicomponent nature of the HIV/SIV receptor has highlighted a problem in terminology. Virus receptors promote binding and entry; however, since CD4 alone allows virus binding but not internalization it is therefore more accurately described as a virus-binding site. CXCR4, CCR5 and other chemokine receptors have variously been termed co-receptors, fusion factors, co-factors, secondary receptors and accessory molecules. It is more appropriate to describe CD4 and the relevant constellation of additional components as a receptor complex in common with other receptor complexes such as the T-cell receptor. CD4 therefore represents the principal binding domain on the receptor complex. The term principal binding receptor that has been used previously for CD4 can be construed as a contradiction in terms.

Now that a greater number of retrovirus receptors have been identified it is becoming increasingly clear that cells resistant to virus infection are not necessarily receptor-negative. The receptor may be present but non-functional. Indeed, cells from different species have probably evolved polymorphic receptors in order to resist infection. Viruses exhibiting receptor interference in cells from one species may therefore not demonstrate receptor interference in cells from a different species.

A new in vitro classification system for HIV based upon chemokine receptor usage has been devised that may supplant previous nomenclatures that have been fraught with anomalies (Berger et al., 1998 ). HIV strains that utilize CCR5 are defined as R5 viruses and viruses that utilize CXCR4 as X4. Viruses that utilize both are R5X4. Viruses that utilize CCR3 or CCR3 and CCR5 are named R3 or R5R3 isolates respectively, to which information as to whether the virus is cell-line adapted or a primary strain is appended. The current classification for HIV, however, does not differentiate between X4 viruses that are CD4-independent and those that are not, probably because a minority of viruses exhibit this feature. Other retroviruses have previously been grouped according to receptor usage (e.g. ALV-A, -B/D, -E and MLV-A, -X, -P, -E) where emphasis is placed on naming the virus. It is clear now that viruses from different animal species may use a common receptor (e.g. FeLV-B and GALV). Considering this complexity of receptor utilization maybe HIV is not the only retrovirus that should be classified with emphasis on naming the receptor. The species of receptor origin should be appended to any receptor nomenclature, since the same receptor expressed in a different cellular context may be non-functional for a particular virus.

Since the cloning of the MLV-E receptor 10 years ago, an impressive number of other retrovirus receptors have been identified (Table 2). Extensive mutagenic analysis of these receptors and the generation of chimeric receptors has shown that defining virus-binding sites on the receptor by a stretch of amino acids can be too simplistic. It is possible that different amino acid sequences may contribute to a surface topology on the receptor that is conducive for virus binding and promoting conformational change in the envelope glycoproteins. Molecules similar in structure to the receptor may potentially function as a scaffold (backbone) and preserve the topology of these sites, when introduced, allowing fusion as has been demonstrated by Lundorf et al. (1999) . Receptors having multiple membrane-spanning domains may highlight a structural requirement for fusion, where the topology of the virus-binding site (conferring a conformational change in viral envelope glycoproteins) is a major determinant of virus specificity and fusion. Although the field of retrovirus receptors has advanced dramatically regarding the identification of virus receptors, the next 10 years may hold the answers as to how they mediate fusion between viral and cellular membranes, permitting particle entry.
